si.t: Reduced emissions from deforestation and degradation
Reduced vulnerability to fire, pests, and diseases
Strengthened social capital and governance capacity
Buffer zone establishment & compatible land use
Enrichment planting with native species (low-medium density)
Riparian buffer & watershed protection within productive landscape
Improved forest productivity and regeneration (soil health)
Increased carbon sequestration and storage (soil); Enhanced resilience to climate hazards
Full reforestation of reclaimable/abandoned converted sites
Hydrology / water management for barren restoration
Increased carbon sequestration and storage (peat carbon especially)
Forest landscape restoration on stable non-forest sites (full reforestation)
Wetland forest restoration (peat swamp / mangrove / riparian)
Sustainable intensification with soil carbon enhancement
Increased carbon sequestration and storage (soil carbon)
Reduced emissions from deforestation and degradation (incentive prevents conversion)
Pre-restoration ecological assessment for stable non-forest/barren sites
Enhanced biodiversity and ecosystem functions (informed planning)
Strengthened social capital and governance capacity (stakeholder consultation as part of assessment)

r.t: Forest Landscape Integrity Index (Grantham et al. 2020)

Direct economic activity: Number of new jobs created

si.t: Forest plantation, Crop plantation, Oil palm, Rubber

Natural forest persists. Conditions in 2024 could be intact OR degraded within forest gradient, but remain forest.
Forest 2014 → Class 1-2 in 2024
Drought Typhoon Landslide Flood High Deforestation risk

- ecosystem preservation (riparrian buffer) - indigenous and local land tenure - community-based forest patrol - participatory disaster risk identification and decision making - establishing PES scheme, NTFP value chain

- ecosystem preservation (mangrove green belts) - indigenous and local land tenure - community-based forest patrol - participatory disaster risk identification and decision making - establishing PES scheme, NTFP value chain, sustainable fishery
- Drought - Typhoon - Flood - High Deforestation risk - Canal development - Peat fires

- ecosystem preservation - indigenous and local land tenure - community-based forest patrol - participatory disaster risk identification and decision making - deep root anchoring - establishing PES scheme, NTFP value chain - hydrological restoration - peat dome protection

Natural forest converted to managed tree cover (Oil palm, Rubber, Forest plantation, Crop plantation)
Forest 2014 → Class 3 in 2024

Forest lost, but vegetation persists (shrub, grass, wetland)
Forest 2014 → Class 4 in 2024

r.t: RESTORE and MANAGE
(threat reduction)

si.t: - vegetation-based restoration (revegetation with native peat swamp species; paludiculture crop) - Peat fire prevention and management - Hydrological restoration

Forest lost to active anthropogenic use (Cropland, Rice, Aquaculture, Settlement)
Forest 2014 → Class 5 in 2024
PROTECT: - Social forestry area establishment - tenurial right acknowledgement
MANAGE-RESTORE: - Associated Mangrove Aquaculture - silvofishery - mangrove belt preservation and/or restoration
MANAGE-PROTECT: - hybrid engineering (mangrove in ports/ ocean highway) - hydrological improvement - installation of trash barrier - invasive species control

PROTECT-RESTORE: - Social forestry area establishment - tenurial right acknowledgement - Hydrological restoration - Vegetation-based restoration (Revegetation with native peat swamp species ; paludiculture) - Peat fire prevention and management

r.t: ASEAN Peatland Management Strategy (APMS) 2023-2030
Global guidelines for peatland rewetting and restoration

si.t: MANAGE-RESTORE: - Sustainable peat water table management/hydrological restoration - High Conservation Value (HCV) peatland protection - Peat fire prevention and management
MANAGE-RESTORE: - Hydrological restoration (canal blocking in non-conservation area/canal backfilling in conservation area)

Severe loss (mining, severe degradation, or cleared but unused)
Forest 2014 → Class 6 in 2024

Small holder and industrial agriculture
Aquaculture
Infrastructure
Mining
Logging
- Ecological Mangrove Rehabilitation - Community based mangrove restoration

MANAGE-RESTORE: - Social forestry area establishment - tenurial right acknowledgement - Hydrological restoration (canal blocking in non-conservation area/canal backfilling in conservation area) - Vegetation-based restoration (Revegetation with native peat swamp species ; paludiculture)

r.t: ASEAN Peatland Management Strategy (APMS) 2023-2030
Global guidelines for peatland rewetting and restoration

si.t: MANAGE-RESTORE: - Sustainable peat water table management/hydrological restoration - High Conservation Value (HCV) peatland protection

MANAGE-RESTORE: - Hydrological restoration (canal blocking in non-conservation area/canal backfilling in conservation area) - Vegetation-based restoration (Revegetation with native peat swamp species ; paludiculture)
- protected area establishment - tenurial acknowledgement - community-based forest patrol -

Plantation stable or plantation established
Non-forest 2014 → Class 3 in 2024

Multiple Possibilities: Long-term stable shrubland/grassland (from earlier forest loss)
Native non-forest vegetation
Stable wetland
Plantation collapse to vegetation
Transition between non-forest types
Non-forest 2014 → Class 4 in 2024
Protect if the same type of ecosystem persists, restore if previous ecosystem was richer

Anthropogenic use, either stable or new establishment from other non-forest
Non-forest 2014 → class 5 2024

Barren land, stable or new
Non-forest 2014 → class 6 2024

activities that increase carbon sequestration and/or reduce GHG by establishing, increasing or restoring vegetative cover (forest or non-forest) through the planting, sowing or human-assisted natural regeneration of woody vegegtation.
May Include timber harvesting in the management plant. Tree planting activities on forest lands managed for wood products are categorized as IFM
non-forest for at least 10 years prior to project
planting, seeding, assisted natural regeneration prioritizing native species

activities that reduce net GHG emissions on croplands and grasslands by increasing carbon stocks in soils and woddy biomass and/or decreasing CO2, N2O and/or CH3 emissions from soils. the project area shall not be cleared of native ecosystems within the 10-year period prior to the project start date
reducing net GHG emissions of cropland systems by increasing soil carbon stocks, reducing soil N2O emissions, and/or reducing CH4 emissions
reducing net GHG emissions of grassland ecosystems by increasing soil carbon stocks, reducing soil N2O emissions, and/or reducing CH4 emissions
changing grazing intensity, improving pasture productivity, nutrient management to sequester carbon in the soil
CGLC - Cropland and Grassland Land-Use Conversions
reducing net GHG emissions by converting cropland to grassland or grassland to cropland, increasing carbon stocks, N2O emissions and/or CH4 emissions
conversion resulting in a net increase carbon stock ( typically soil)

Increasing carbon sequestration and/or reduce GHG emissions on forest lands managed for wood products (sawtimber, pulpwood and fuelwood) by increasing biomass carbon stocks through improving forest management practices.

baseline and project scenarios for the project area shall qualify as forests remaining as forests, and the project area shall be designated, sanctioned or approved for wood product management by a national or local regulatory body (e.g. logging concessions or plantations)
switching from convetional logging to RIL during timber harvesting
RIL-C, reducing damage to residual trees and soil during harvest
permanently converting logged forests to protected forests by eliminating harvesting for timber, biomass carboon stocks are protected and can increase as the forest regrows and/or continue to grow.
harvesting of trees to advance conservation purposes (removal of diseased trees) may continue in the project scenario
stopping commercial logging, permanently protecting the area as conservation forest
extending the rotation age or cutting cycle and increasing carbon stocks
delaying harvest beyond standard commercial rotation age
LtHP - Low Productive to High Productive Forest
converting low-productivity forests to high-productivity forests.
silvicultural improvements to increase forests' productivity and carbon sequestration rate
permanently stoping or reducing deforestation on forest lands that are legally authorized and documented for conversion.
must provide documented proof that the forest is legally sanctioned to be cleared for other uses in the near future
stopping deforestation and/or degradation of degraded to mature forests that would have occured in any forest configuration
must be justified through the jurisdictional risk maps, proving the area is in a high-threat zone
stopping conversions of grasslands or shrublands that are legally authorized and documented for conversion
stopping unplanned conversions of grasslands or shrublands
enhancing, creating and/or managing hydrological conditions, sediment supply, salinity characteristics , water quality and./or native plant communities.
avoiding degradation and/or the conversion of wetlands that are intact or partially altered while still maintaining their natural function
eligible activity: APWD - Avoiding Planned Wetland Degradation AUWD - Avoiding Unplanned Wetland Degradation
Deforestation risk ≥ Medium OR Fire risk ≥ Medium
Reduced vulnerability to fire, pests, and diseases; Protection of watershed functions
Sustainable livelihood opportunities; Strengthened social capital and governance capacity
Secure land and resource tenure; Cultural heritage preservation; Strengthened social capital and governance capacity; Equitable benefit-sharing mechanisms
Enhanced food and water security; Strengthened social capital and governance capacity
Reduced emissions from deforestation and degradation; Enhanced resilience to climate hazards
Maintenance of ecological connectivity; Reduced vulnerability to fire, pests, and diseases
Sustainable livelihood opportunities; Equitable benefit-sharing mechanisms
Reduced emissions from deforestation and degradation; Microclimate regulation
Landslide risk ≥ Medium OR Flash flood ≥ Medium
Improved forest productivity and regeneration; Protection of watershed functions
Increased carbon sequestration and storage; Enhanced resilience to climate hazards
Reduced vulnerability to fire, pests, and diseases; Improved forest productivity and regeneration
Enhanced biodiversity and ecosystem functions; Improved forest productivity and regeneration; Maintenance of ecological connectivity
Increased carbon sequestration and storage; Reduced emissions from deforestation and degradation
Enhanced biodiversity and ecosystem functions; Improved forest productivity and regeneration
Sustainable livelihood opportunities (planting employment); Enhanced food and water security
Increased carbon sequestration and storage; Microclimate regulation
Category 4: Forest converted to active use
Sustainable livelihood opportunities; Enhanced food and water security
Increased carbon sequestration and storage; Enhanced resilience to climate hazards; Microclimate regulation
Flood risk ≥ Medium OR Flash flood ≥ Medium OR presence of water body
Protection of watershed functions; Maintenance of ecological connectivity
Increased carbon sequestration and storage (riparian); Enhanced resilience to climate hazards; Microclimate regulation
Presence of water body within or adjacent to site OR Flood risk ≥ Medium
Protection of watershed functions; Maintenance of ecological connectivity; Enhanced biodiversity and ecosystem functions
Improved forest productivity and regeneration; Enhanced biodiversity and ecosystem functions
Protection of watershed functions; Improved forest productivity and regeneration
Increased carbon sequestration and storage (peat carbon especially); Enhanced resilience to climate hazards
ANY (when assessment confirms reference = forest AND site not in active use)
Protection of watershed functions; Enhanced biodiversity and ecosystem functions; Maintenance of ecological connectivity
Category 9: Active use stable or established
Peatland: paludiculture intensification with hydrology safeguard; Mangrove: silvofishery intensification
Improved forest productivity and regeneration (soil health); Enhanced biodiversity and ecosystem functions
Increased carbon sequestration and storage (soil carbon); Enhanced resilience to climate hazards
Sustainable livelihood opportunities; Equitable benefit-sharing mechanisms; Strengthened social capital and governance capacity
forest law enforcement reduced impact logging assisted natural regenaration village no-expansion agreement Incident reporting and escalation SOP (document logging and/or forest clearing activities)
assisted natural regenaration in degraded area sediment control

- ecosystem preservation (riparrian buffer, mangrove green belts, sediment accretion management) - indigenous and local land tenure - community-based forest patrol - participatory disaster risk identification and decision making - hydrological rewetting - peat dome protection - establishing PES scheme, NTFP value chain, sustainable fishery, paludiculture crop - deep root anchoring

r.t: https://www.gfdrr.org/en/publication/nature-based-solutions-disaster-risk-management-booklet
https://rightsandresources.org/impact/tenure-and-conservation/
https://www.preventionweb.net/publication/words-action-guidelines-nature-based-solutions-disaster-risk-reduction

si.t: agriculture land management, improved cropland, improved grassland management

- ecological corridords - hybrid engineering (mangrove in ports/ocean highway) - water flow maintenance under structure - local community involvement through participatory decision making and capacity building

r.t: https://www.unep.org/explore-topics/infrastructure/about-sustainable-infrastructure-partnership
https://www.giz.de/en/worldwide/75452.html
http://teebweb.org/areas-of-work/teeb-for-business/

si.t: Tree planting / reforestation planting Fire exclusion (pertanian tanpa bakar) Enrichment planting
Fire control Fire exclusion (pertanian tanpa bakar) Gradual succession
Soil recovery Riparian buffer restoration Enrichment planting
IPCC AFOLU and water-system guidance; riparian restoration literature or national watershed rules
Assisted natural regeneration Enrichment planting Gradual succession
Greening Program in Berakas Forest Reserve
Promotion of native species and removal of invasives
Reduced erosion through improved soil structure and fertility
Establishment of protected areas for the protection of critical habitats and biodiversity
Evaluating the Impact of Nature-based Solutions
Increased stewardship and knowledge transfer through local participation
[Modified] Knowledge and Social Capacity Building for Sustainable Transformation
Community involvement in environmental education activities
New Economic Opportunities and Green Jobs
Alternative sustainable livelihoods and income source
[Modified] New Economic Opportunities and Green Jobs
Indirect economic activity: number of new businesses established related to NBS
NBS cost/benefit analysis: Initial costs, Maintenance costs, Replacement cost
Protection of key species habitats (e.g. bees) and associated pollination services
Extent of habitat for native pollinator species
Build upon Indigenous knowledge with standard harvesting rules
Increased household income and reduced poverty
Direct economic activity: Gross Value Added to local economy from new business creation
Direct economic activity: Retail and commercial activity
Indirect economic activity: New customers to businesses in proximity to NBS
Prevention of deforestation and habitat degradation
Forest habitat fragmentation - effective mesh density
Strengthened cultural identity and spiritual connection with nature
Inclusive governance that respects traditions and traditional ecological knowledge
Village-Driven Forest Rehabilitation and Livelihood Improvement
Forest restoration for 66 ha across 4 target villages
Enhancement of key ecosystem services (e.g., water regulation)
Infiltration rate / Flood peak reduction / Rainfall interception of NBS
Gazettement of 37,613 ha of Stateland Forest to Permanent Forest Reserve
Rehabilitation of 839 ha of degraded forests
Preservation of habitats for endangered species
Increased household income for Orang Asli communities involved in the Community-based Organization projects
Increased attention of international funding and investments
Social and Ecological Impacts of Agroforestry in Yangon Region
Increased tree species richness, evenness, and abundance (i.e., tree diversity)
Biodiversity Index (H’), Evenness Index (e) and Species Domination Index (C)
Higher number of mature tree species (71-80 cm diameter class)
Stages of forest stand development -Number of class diameter
Increased community participation in community forest activity
Significant increase in on-farm income from agroforestry and natural forest conservation
Community-based forest management - comprehensive agrarian reform program (CBFM-CARP)
Increased forest cover and species diversity
Spawning grounds for aquatic species in mangrove areas
Sequestration of 1,196 million tons of carbon documented in the CBFM assessment in 2018-2019
Measured soil carbon content; Modelled carbon content; Soil organic matter content; Soil organic matter index
Increased access to utilities and facilities
Increase in over 50% in annual income (2008-2012)
Restoration of coastal forests and mangroves
Creation of buffers for criticat habitats
Forest habitat fragmentation – effective mesh density
Reduction in sea level rise risk and erosion
Total predicted soil loss; Area exposed to flood risk
Potential reduction in damage to recreational areas
Reduced sediment flow to the sea
Species diversity within a defined area
Increased recreational space in mangrove areas
Enhanced water management during drought and wet periods
Net surface water availability; Quantitative status of groundwater; Effective Drought Index; Standardised Precipitation Index
Green spaces for social interactions and recreational activities
Soil water holding capacity; Plant-available water; Soil Available Water (SAW) for plant uptake
Potential job opportunities for local communities involved in the maintenance and monitoring of the park
Enhanced ecosystem services (e.g., steady water supply)
Emphasis of local knowledge in processing forest product
Enhanced ecosystem services, suh as pollination services for bees
Fostered greater gender equality with women participation
Higher income from organic coffee and coffee-derived products
Annual added value of ~1,050 million baht to the project area
Mainstreaming EbA into national climate change adaptation strategy and land use and development
Pilot restoration of coastal protection forests in Quang Binh
Area exposed to drought and flood risk
Systematic integration of ecosystem-based approach in Viet Nam's policies
Pilot project on the diversification of livelihood sources in Ha Tinh
Reduction in illegal logging during active patrol

r.t: https://media.afocosec.org/2020/07/AFoCO-5.-Monitoring-and-evaluation-plan-EN.pdf

si.t: Capacity building for data collection and monitoring
Safeguard source of livelihood for ~200,000 people
Overall economic, social and health well-being
